video_title: The New U.S. Navy Shipbuilding Plan - Nuclear Battleships, FF(X) & America's Naval Buildup
gist: Analysis of the US Navy's FY2027 30-year shipbuilding plan: a record-breaking, sub-budget being consumed by the new $14-17B nuclear-powered Trump-class battleship (USS Defiant) at the expense of frigates and other firepower, while submarines remain the dominant spend and DDG(X) is quietly cancelled. Net effect over most of the plan is FEWER battle-force ships than the prior FY2025 plan despite far more money, with heavy execution risk around industrial base and Congressional pushback.

insights.claim: The US Navy's FY2027 30-year shipbuilding plan raises battle-force shipbuilding investment from sub-$40B (FY2025) to north of $60B in FY2027, 2028, 2030 and 2031 (with FY2029 dipping to ~$50B), a record-breaking shipbuilding budget.
insights.speaker: Perun (host)
insights.so_what: A durable double-digit-percent uplift in naval procurement spend is a multi-decade tailwind for US naval primes and the shipbuilding supply chain; watch for the annual Congressional shipbuilding markup that historically adds even more.

insights.claim: Despite spending far more money, the FY2027 plan would have the US Navy operating FEWER battle-force ships through most of the 2030s and all of the 2040s than the FY2025 plan, with both plans converging on ~400 ships in 2056 (up from 291 today).
insights.speaker: Perun (host)
insights.so_what: More dollars buying fewer hulls means spend concentrates in a few high-unit-cost programs (battleship, subs); investors should track per-unit cost inflation rather than fleet headcount as the growth signal.
insights.quote: how do you plan to spend a shit ton more money and end up with fewer boats?

insights.claim: The shipbuilding plan is a statement of intent, not an approved budget; the US Congress controls funding and has consistently voted up MORE shipbuilding money than the White House/Navy requested, and has legislated a minimum carrier count.
insights.speaker: Perun (host)
insights.so_what: Congressional adds are a recurring upside catalyst for shipbuilders; the plan sets a floor, not a ceiling, for procurement-exposed names.

insights.claim: Over the 5-year horizon the plan spends more on nuclear submarines than on carriers, battleships, destroyers and frigates COMBINED, confirming the submarine force as the structural priority of the US Navy budget.
insights.speaker: Perun (host)
insights.so_what: Submarine-exposed primes and their suppliers (General Dynamics Electric Boat, HII Newport News) capture the largest and most durable share of naval procurement dollars.

insights.claim: The Trump-class guided-missile battleship (USS Defiant) is now expected to cost ~$17B per hull (vs an earlier ~$9B estimate), with $43.5B requested through FY2031 for 3 ships (~$14.5B average) — as or more expensive than a nuclear aircraft carrier nearly three times its displacement.
insights.speaker: Perun (host)
insights.so_what: A single ~$17B program line is the marginal budget swing factor for the whole plan; any builder winning the battleship contract gets an enormous backlog, but the program is the most likely cancellation/de-scope candidate.
insights.quote: they don't actually expect Defiant to cost $9 billion, they expect it to cost closer to $17 billion

insights.claim: The battleship has been switched from conventional CODAG to NUCLEAR propulsion using the existing A1B reactor from the Ford-class carrier (steam generator, pressuriser, loop and coolant-pump components), reusing an existing reactor design and supply chain.
insights.speaker: Chief of Naval Operations (admiral, quoted)
insights.so_what: Reusing the A1B reactor concentrates demand on the existing naval-reactor supply chain (BWX Technologies for naval nuclear components) and limits build to nuclear-capable yards (HII Newport News, GD Electric Boat).
insights.quote: We are going to use the Ford reactor, the A1B reactor, and the components that support that from the vessel

insights.claim: Going nuclear narrows the battleship build to the small set of US shipyards and workforces with nuclear capability, worsening already-acute schedule and capacity risk for the program.
insights.speaker: Perun (host)
insights.so_what: Nuclear-capable yard capacity is the binding constraint; the same yards already strained on carriers and submarines now face battleship demand, a bottleneck (and pricing-power) signal for incumbents.

insights.claim: The plan procures the first battleship in FY2028 and builds toward a peak of 11 in service by 2055, putting the Navy on a roughly 1:1 battleship-to-carrier ratio in the 2050s; over 5 years battleships absorb more funding than destroyers and frigates combined and nearly twice the carrier allocation.
insights.speaker: Perun (host)
insights.so_what: The battleship cannibalizes the rest of the surface-combatant budget; if cancelled, freed funds redirect to Burkes, frigates, drones and munitions — making it a binary swing factor across multiple supplier segments.

insights.claim: On a cost-for-cost and ton-for-ton basis the battleship is a poor way to add missile firepower: Defiant is outgunned roughly 2:1 to 3:1 in VLS cells by a trio of Arleigh Burkes or Chinese Type 055 cruisers at similar or lower displacement and price.
insights.speaker: Perun (host)
insights.so_what: The capability-per-dollar critique underpins the cancellation thesis; the strategic logic favors more Burkes/frigates/drones over the battleship, where the bulk of efficient firepower spend would otherwise flow.

insights.claim: Congress has reportedly moved to block the battleship program until all its weapon systems (railgun, hypersonics, directed-energy weapons) reach sufficient technical readiness — which, given those systems are largely experimental, could effectively kill it.
insights.speaker: Perun (host)
insights.so_what: A concrete near-term legislative risk to the largest line item; battleship-exposed contractors face binary program risk tied to immature-tech gating and the 2028 administration change.

themes.theme: Nuclear submarine force — the budget core

insights.claim: The plan orders 10 Virginia-class attack submarines over 5 years with per-unit cost falling from ~$7B (FY2027) to ~$5.6B (FY2031), ~$6.3B average; at 2 boats/year ($62.9B over 5 years) Virginia is the single most expensive line item in the entire plan.
insights.speaker: Perun (host)
insights.so_what: Virginia is the largest and most stable revenue stream in naval procurement; the GD Electric Boat / HII Newport News teaming and their suppliers are the prime beneficiaries.

insights.claim: The Columbia-class ballistic-missile submarine remains the top procurement priority at one per year, ~$12.4B per unit and ~$62B over 5 years, replacing the Ohio SSBN fleet over the plan; cost is in line with the UK Dreadnought and France's next SSBN.
insights.speaker: Perun (host)
insights.so_what: Columbia is a protected, must-fund nuclear-deterrence program — the most schedule-critical and politically insulated backlog for GD Electric Boat and its supply chain.

insights.claim: After retiring all SSGN Tomahawk-cruise-missile submarines (154 cells each) in FY2027-2029, the plan restarts SSGN procurement in FY2038 at one every 3 years — timed 2-3 years after the last Columbia, hinting the Columbia production line/workforce would be kept warm with a large-payload follow-on boat.
insights.speaker: Perun (host)
insights.so_what: Signals a continuous, decades-long large-hull submarine workload for the Columbia yard beyond 2035 — extending the duration of GD Electric Boat's submarine backlog.

insights.claim: The next-gen SSN(X) attack sub (entering service mid-2040s) is being designed from the ground up to host robotic and autonomous undersea systems, positioning each boat as a control node for cheaper unmanned underwater vehicles.
insights.speaker: Perun (host)
insights.so_what: Long-dated optionality for undersea-autonomy suppliers and the UUV ecosystem; analogous to NGAD's manned-node-plus-drones concept for the Air Force.

insights.claim: The binding risk on the entire submarine plan is industrial capacity, not design quality: 2 attack boats per year has been a stated goal for years and delays are the norm rather than the exception.
insights.speaker: Perun (host)
insights.so_what: Submarine-yard throughput and workforce/supplier scaling are the key execution variables; investments in submarine-industrial-base expansion (suppliers, casting, workforce) are a watch-list theme.

insights.claim: The Constellation-class (FFG-X) frigate program was cancelled after just two ships (CBO estimated ~$1.4B/hull), replaced by FF(X), a navalized Coast Guard Legend-class cutter that drops the 32-cell VLS, Aegis combat system, high-end sensors and sonar.
insights.speaker: Perun (host)
insights.so_what: Constellation's cancellation removes a major Fincantieri Marinette Marine backlog and shifts frigate work to whichever yard builds the cutter-derived FF(X); a clear loser/winner reshuffle in small-combatant builders.

insights.claim: FF(X) is described as cheap but actually costs ~$1.75B per hull ($7B+ for 4 over 5 years, peaking at $2.76B in FY2031) — more than the cancelled Constellation it replaces, while delivering less capability and arriving later.
insights.speaker: Perun (host)
insights.so_what: The 'cost-effective' frigate is neither cheaper nor faster, undermining the savings rationale; expect program churn and possible re-scoping (VLS/ASW added back), which is execution and margin risk for the frigate builder.
insights.quote: a small surface combatant that can't really do surface combat, a cheap ship that is actually expensive, and a fast to deliver option that actually arrives later than the slow to deliver one it replaced

insights.claim: Swapping Constellation (32-cell VLS) for FF(X) (no VLS) strips ~2,600 VLS cells from the future fleet; total fleet missile cells barely stay net-positive only by counting the battleship's ~1,900 added cells and containerized/hypersonic add-ons.
insights.speaker: Perun (host)
insights.so_what: Despite record spend, fleet vertical-launch capacity barely grows — a structural argument for sustained munitions/VLS demand and for missile-magazine alternatives (unmanned magazine ships).

insights.claim: Allied frigate designs (UK Type 26/Type 31, French FDI/FREMM, Japanese Mogami) deliver VLS and ASW at similar or lower cost than FF(X); adopting one (e.g., Type 26) would create industrial commonality with the UK, Canada and Australia.
insights.speaker: Perun (host)
insights.so_what: An off-the-shelf allied design pivot would benefit BAE/Naval Group/MHI ecosystems and AUKUS-aligned supply chains; a latent optionality if FF(X) falters.

insights.claim: The Ford-class carrier is now treated as a 'battle-proven design' (10,500 fixed-wing launch/recoveries on its June-2025 deployment) and the plan keeps procuring them, while the carrier force stays roughly static in numbers over 30 years.
insights.speaker: Perun (host)
insights.so_what: Steady, de-risked carrier procurement underpins HII Newport News's long-cycle backlog without large fleet-count growth.

insights.claim: The next-generation destroyer DDG(X) has been entirely deleted from the plan — absent from designs, budget and build list — with the battleship explicitly stated NOT to be a destroyer replacement, leaving the 1980s-era Arleigh Burke (procured 1-2/year through the 2050s) as the only destroyer.
insights.speaker: Perun (host)
insights.so_what: DDG(X) cancellation extends the Arleigh Burke production line (good for HII Ingalls/GD Bath Iron Works) but leaves a long-term destroyer-modernization and hypersonic-cell gap that may require a future program restart.
insights.quote: in this shipbuilding plan DDG(X) is nowhere to be seen

insights.claim: Killing DDG(X) removes the planned hypersonic-capable destroyer fleet, likely leaving far fewer Conventional Prompt Strike hypersonic cells in the future force than previously planned despite hypersonics being a stated priority.
insights.speaker: Perun (host)
insights.so_what: Fewer surface hypersonic platforms concentrates that capability on Zumwalts and submarines; a watch item for hypersonic-weapon program demand allocation.

insights.claim: Unmanned systems are funded but dwarfed by the battleship: $1.1B for 16 XLUUVs (Orca) over 5 years (less than one FF(X)) and $3.1B for 47 medium unmanned surface vessels, with no German-style armed 'magazine-extender' uncrewed missile vessel in the plan.
insights.speaker: Perun (host)
insights.so_what: This is not an unmanned-centric budget; the gap versus Ukraine/Iran-era lessons suggests upside for low-cost unmanned and missile-magazine concepts if priorities shift, and underweight current exposure for USV/UUV suppliers.
insights.quote: This is not a revolution in naval shipbuilding so much as an incremental step in it.

insights.claim: US military shipbuilding is the bedrock of the domestic yards because the US now builds only a fraction of a percent of global civilian ships (bulk carriers/LNG tankers ceded to Japan, South Korea and China), so Navy/Coast Guard plans are effectively the entire demand signal for large-ship yards.
insights.speaker: Perun (host)
insights.so_what: US yard revenue is almost entirely government-driven, making these primes pure-play defense-budget exposures with little commercial offset; allied (Korean/Japanese) yards are the natural partners for any capacity expansion.

insights.claim: The combat-logistics and support-vessel category (oilers, etc.) sees a front-loaded surge between FY2027 and 2037, one of the few fast-growing fleet segments early in the plan.
insights.speaker: Perun (host)
insights.so_what: Near-term growth in auxiliary/support shipbuilding offers earlier revenue than the slow-ramping combatant programs — a less-watched beneficiary segment.

insights.claim: The plan's central trade-off: cutting the battleship at constant funding could instead buy ~7 additional Arleigh Burkes plus an extra carrier with cash to spare, or refill magazines and fund a large ASW frigate fleet plus unmanned systems — the Navy cannot fund both the battleship and a broad force refresh.
insights.speaker: Perun (host)
insights.so_what: Frames the battleship as a binary capital-allocation switch across the whole supplier base; cancellation would broadly benefit Burke builders, munitions makers, frigate and unmanned suppliers, and naval air (F/A-XX).
insights.quote: the battleship is the trade, and a lot of other things get the off

insights.claim: Perun's base case is that the battleship is most likely eventually cancelled or seriously revised, with risk that an administration change at end-2028 wastes billions and years of DDG(X) opportunity cost, leaving the Defiants a tiny Zumwalt/Constellation-like orphan class.
insights.speaker: Perun (host)
insights.so_what: Treat battleship-linked revenue as high-risk/optional and the DDG(X) gap as a likely future restart; position for redirection of funds rather than full battleship execution.

insights.claim: Funding remains unsettled for key US naval-air programs including the F/A-XX 6th-generation fighter and various Navy unmanned aircraft, which compete against the shipbuilding battleship spend.
insights.speaker: Perun (host)
insights.so_what: Battleship cost growth pressures the broader Navy aviation budget; F/A-XX program timing/funding is a contested catalyst for naval-air primes.
